content.text: Model selection agents automate the systematic evaluation of competing ML algorithms or architectures for a given task. Where hyperparameter optimization agents explore the configuration space of a fixed model family, model selection agents navigate the broader choice of which model family to use at all — should the team build a random forest, an XGBoost tree ensemble, a neural network, or a linear model? Each choice has different performance characteristics, latency profiles, interpretability properties, and infrastructure requirements. Choosing well requires testing each candidate on the actual task data, not just trusting published benchmark numbers.

A model selection agent formalizes this process by accepting a task specification (problem type, evaluation metric, latency budget, interpretability constraints, infrastructure cost limit), enumerating a candidate set of models, evaluating each against the specification through cross-validation, and applying Pareto optimization to find the model that best satisfies the objectives jointly. The agent makes the selection decision transparent and repeatable — not an artifact of which model a team member happened to try first.

attrs.alt: Model selection agent architecture: task specification feeds agent that enumerates candidates, evaluates each, applies Pareto optimization on performance and latency constraints, and outputs a ranked recommendation

content.text: A model selection agent is a decision-making system that given a task specification (problem type, success metric, constraints), evaluates a set of candidate models through systematic cross-validation and returns a ranked recommendation with justification. It differs from AutoML in scope: AutoML typically searches hyperparameters and architecture jointly in an end-to-end pipeline; a model selection agent focuses on the higher-level choice of algorithm family before detailed tuning begins. The agent applies explicit constraint logic — a model that violates the latency budget is removed from consideration regardless of its accuracy — and uses Pareto dominance to identify the efficient frontier among non-dominated candidates.

Without a structured selection process, teams default to familiar models regardless of fit. A team skilled in deep learning will reach for a neural network for a tabular classification task where XGBoost would train in 1% of the time and achieve equal accuracy. A team that always starts with logistic regression will miss 15% accuracy improvements available from a more expressive model. The selection decision also has lasting consequences: the chosen model architecture determines interpretability, serving infrastructure, retraining complexity, and debuggability for months. A model selection agent enforces discipline — every candidate is evaluated against the same holdout with the same protocol.
Eliminates selection bias: all candidates evaluated with identical folds, preprocessing, and metrics.
Constraint enforcement: models that violate latency, memory, or interpretability requirements are pruned before the comparison, not as an afterthought.
Multi-objective awareness: Pareto optimization surfaces the full trade-off between accuracy and latency rather than collapsing it into a single score.
Meta-learning warm start: if the team has evaluated models on previous similar datasets, the agent uses that history to prioritize likely-good candidates.
Auditability: the selection decision is logged with the evaluation metrics, candidate set, and constraint specification — reviewable in the future.
Compute cost: cross-validating 10 model families with default hyperparameters times 5 folds is 50 training jobs. Worth it, but requires a compute allocation.
Default hyperparameters: the agent selects based on default or lightly tuned hyperparameters per candidate. The winning model might not be the global optimum — HPO on the winner is the next step.
Dataset shift: a model selected on historical data may not remain optimal when data distribution changes. Re-run selection periodically, not just once.

The agent operates in three phases. First, candidate pruning: the full candidate set is filtered to models that are feasible under the hard constraints (latency, memory, interpretability requirement). This happens before any training. Second, evaluation: each feasible candidate is trained and evaluated using stratified k-fold cross-validation on the task data. The agent records mean and standard deviation of the target metric across folds, plus inference latency on a representative sample. Third, recommendation: the agent computes the Pareto front over (metric, latency) and returns the non-dominated candidates, highlighting the one that maximizes the metric subject to the latency constraint.

The agent has a registry of candidate models, each described by its algorithm class, default hyperparameters, interpretability score, and known performance priors from meta-learning databases like OpenML. The evaluation engine parallelizes across candidates using joblib or Ray. Results are stored in a comparison table indexed by (candidate, fold). The Pareto optimizer filters and ranks the comparison table. The output is a recommendation object with the winning candidate, its cross-validation metrics, the second-best alternative (useful for sensitivity analysis), and the reasons other candidates were eliminated.
Starting a new ML project where the team does not already have strong prior knowledge about which algorithms work on this data type.
Procurement or compliance contexts where the selection decision must be documented and auditable.
Multi-objective settings where accuracy and latency or interpretability must be traded off explicitly.
After a significant data pipeline change that may alter which model family works best.

"""Model selection agent with Pareto optimization and constraint enforcement."""
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any, Dict, List, Optional
import time
import numpy as np
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification
from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold, cross_val_score
from sklearn.metrics import f1_score, make_scorer
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# Try importing xgboost; skip if not available
try:
    from xgboost import XGBClassifier
    HAS_XGB = True
except ImportError:
    HAS_XGB = False


@dataclass
class TaskSpec:
    problem_type: str = "classification"
    metric: str = "f1_macro"
    max_latency_ms: float = 20.0
    require_interpretable: bool = False
    n_cv_folds: int = 5


@dataclass
class CandidateResult:
    name: str
    mean_metric: float
    std_metric: float
    latency_ms: float
    interpretable: bool
    feasible: bool = True
    rejection_reason: Optional[str] = None


def measure_latency(model, X_sample: np.ndarray, n_repeats: int = 50) -> float:
    """Return mean single-sample inference latency in milliseconds."""
    times = []
    for _ in range(n_repeats):
        start = time.perf_counter()
        model.predict(X_sample[:1])
        times.append((time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000)
    return float(np.mean(times))


def build_candidates() -> Dict[str, Any]:
    candidates = {
        "logistic_regression": Pipeline([
            ("scaler", StandardScaler()),
            ("clf", LogisticRegression(max_iter=500, random_state=0)),
        ]),
        "random_forest": RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=0, n_jobs=-1),
        "gradient_boosting": GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=0),
    }
    if HAS_XGB:
        candidates["xgboost"] = XGBClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=0,
                                               use_label_encoder=False, eval_metric="logloss")
    return candidates


INTERPRETABLE = {"logistic_regression", "random_forest"}  # RF is inspectable via feature importance


def run_model_selection(X: np.ndarray, y: np.ndarray, spec: TaskSpec) -> List[CandidateResult]:
    candidates = build_candidates()
    scorer = make_scorer(f1_score, average="macro")
    cv = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=spec.n_cv_folds, shuffle=True, random_state=0)
    results = []

    for name, model in candidates.items():
        interpretable = name in INTERPRETABLE
        # Hard constraint: interpretability
        if spec.require_interpretable and not interpretable:
            results.append(CandidateResult(
                name=name, mean_metric=0.0, std_metric=0.0, latency_ms=0.0,
                interpretable=interpretable, feasible=False,
                rejection_reason="interpretability constraint",
            ))
            continue

        # Cross-validate
        scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=cv, scoring=scorer, n_jobs=-1)
        model.fit(X, y)  # fit on full data for latency measurement
        latency = measure_latency(model, X)

        # Hard constraint: latency
        if latency > spec.max_latency_ms:
            results.append(CandidateResult(
                name=name,
                mean_metric=float(np.mean(scores)),
                std_metric=float(np.std(scores)),
                latency_ms=latency,
                interpretable=interpretable,
                feasible=False,
                rejection_reason=f"latency {latency:.1f}ms > budget {spec.max_latency_ms}ms",
            ))
            continue

        results.append(CandidateResult(
            name=name,
            mean_metric=float(np.mean(scores)),
            std_metric=float(np.std(scores)),
            latency_ms=latency,
            interpretable=interpretable,
        ))

    # Sort feasible candidates by metric descending
    feasible = [r for r in results if r.feasible]
    infeasible = [r for r in results if not r.feasible]
    feasible.sort(key=lambda r: r.mean_metric, reverse=True)
    return feasible + infeasible


# Demo
X, y = make_classification(n_samples=3000, n_features=20, random_state=0)
spec = TaskSpec(max_latency_ms=50.0, require_interpretable=False)
results = run_model_selection(X, y, spec)

print(f"\n{'Name':<25} {'F1 Mean':>10} {'F1 Std':>8} {'Lat(ms)':>9} {'Feasible':>10}")
for r in results:
    reason = f" ({r.rejection_reason})" if not r.feasible else ""
    print(f"{r.name:<25} {r.mean_metric:>10.4f} {r.std_metric:>8.4f} {r.latency_ms:>9.2f} {str(r.feasible):>10}{reason}")
print(f"\nRecommendation: {results[0].name} (F1={results[0].mean_metric:.4f})")

"""Compute the Pareto front over (F1, -latency) to identify efficient candidates."""
from typing import List
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class EvalResult:
    name: str
    f1: float
    latency_ms: float


def is_pareto_dominated(candidate: EvalResult, others: List[EvalResult]) -> bool:
    """Return True if candidate is dominated by any other result."""
    for other in others:
        if other.name == candidate.name:
            continue
        # other dominates candidate if it is at least as good on both objectives
        # and strictly better on at least one
        better_f1 = other.f1 >= candidate.f1
        better_lat = other.latency_ms <= candidate.latency_ms
        strictly_better = (other.f1 > candidate.f1) or (other.latency_ms < candidate.latency_ms)
        if better_f1 and better_lat and strictly_better:
            return True
    return False


def pareto_front(results: List[EvalResult]) -> List[EvalResult]:
    return [r for r in results if not is_pareto_dominated(r, results)]


# Example with realistic numbers
results = [
    EvalResult("logistic_regression", f1=0.82, latency_ms=1.5),
    EvalResult("random_forest",       f1=0.87, latency_ms=4.2),
    EvalResult("xgboost",             f1=0.93, latency_ms=8.1),
    EvalResult("neural_network",      f1=0.96, latency_ms=45.0),
    EvalResult("svm",                 f1=0.85, latency_ms=12.0),  # dominated by RF and XGB
]

front = pareto_front(results)
print("Pareto-optimal candidates:")
for r in sorted(front, key=lambda x: x.f1):
    print(f"  {r.name:<25} F1={r.f1:.3f}  latency={r.latency_ms:.1f}ms")

"""After selecting a model, compute SHAP feature importance for interpretability."""
import shap
import numpy as np
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

X, y = make_classification(n_samples=1000, n_features=10, random_state=0)
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=0)

feature_names = [f"feat_{i}" for i in range(X.shape[1])]

model = GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=0)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Tree SHAP is exact and fast for tree-based models
explainer = shap.TreeExplainer(model)
shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X_test)

# Mean absolute SHAP = global feature importance
mean_shap = np.abs(shap_values).mean(axis=0)
importance = sorted(zip(feature_names, mean_shap), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

print("Feature importance (mean |SHAP|):")
for name, score in importance:
    bar = "#" * int(score * 50)
    print(f"  {name:<12} {score:.4f} {bar}")

A credit risk team at a bank needed to replace an aging logistic regression scorecard. Regulatory guidance required model decisions to be explainable to customers (a GDPR Article 22 obligation). The team ran a model selection agent over six candidates: logistic regression, decision tree, random forest, gradient boosting, LightGBM, and a neural network. The agent enforced two hard constraints: inference latency under 5ms (to meet real-time credit decisioning SLAs) and a minimum SHAP-based interpretability score. Neural networks failed the interpretability constraint; LightGBM passed both constraints and achieved the highest F1. The selection process ran in 45 minutes and produced a comparison table that was included in the model governance document submitted to the risk committee.
The model selection comparison must use cross-validation on the training set only. Using test data for selection introduces selection bias — the test estimate will be optimistic.
If the serving pipeline includes a scaler, encoder, or feature computation step, include those in the latency measurement. The model's raw predict() call is not the deployment latency.
A model optimized for overall accuracy may perform poorly on the rare but important class. Always include per-class metrics and check calibration on minority classes.

If the F1 difference between two candidates is 0.01 with standard deviation 0.02 across folds, the difference is not statistically meaningful. Report mean ± std and use paired t-tests for close calls.
Always start with the constraint check before any training — eliminate infeasible candidates immediately to save compute.
Include at least one simple baseline (logistic regression or linear model) in the candidate set. If a complex model does not beat the baseline significantly, the baseline is the right choice.
Use the same random seed for all candidates to make cross-validation fold splits identical — this reduces variance in the comparison.
After selecting the winner, run HPO on the winner only. Avoid running HPO on all candidates — that is AutoML, not model selection.
Document the rejection reasons for each eliminated candidate. Future team members will ask why you did not use a neural network, and the answer should be traceable.

Model selection with 10 candidates and 5-fold cross-validation requires 50 model fits. For fast models (linear, shallow trees) this completes in minutes on a single core. For slow models (deep GBT, SVM on large data) each fit may take 30 minutes, making the full selection run take 25 hours sequentially. Parallelize across candidates and folds using joblib with

or Ray for distributed evaluation. For very large datasets, run selection on a stratified subsample (10–20%) of the training data — the relative ordering of candidates is usually stable at subsample sizes above 10k rows.
Full AutoML that includes model selection and HPO via Bayesian optimization. Good for getting a strong baseline quickly.
Fast and lightweight AutoML from Microsoft, strong on tabular data, very low overhead.
High-level ML library that automates model comparison with a single API call. Good for rapid prototyping.
Repository of datasets and evaluation results that powers meta-learning priors for model selection.
Model evaluation handles arbitrary candidate objects. If candidates come from external sources (plugins, user-submitted code), run each in a sandbox process to prevent malicious code execution.

Log all constraint checks and their outcomes. If a constraint is later updated (e.g., the latency budget changes from 20ms to 50ms), the selection must be re-run — constraint changes are not retroactively applied.
For regulated domains: preserve the comparison table, cross-validation folds (seed), and dataset version as part of the model card. Auditors will ask to see why a specific model was chosen.
Model selection agents evaluate competing algorithm families systematically rather than relying on team familiarity or intuition.
Hard constraints (latency, interpretability, cost) must be enforced before the accuracy comparison — not treated as secondary concerns.
Pareto optimization surfaces the efficient frontier between accuracy and latency, making trade-offs explicit rather than implicit.
Always include a simple baseline (logistic regression) in the candidate set — it sets the minimum bar for model complexity.
Selection uses cross-validation on training data only; the test set is never seen during selection.
After selection, apply HPO to the winner — selection uses default or lightly tuned hyperparameters, not the optimal configuration.
Document rejection reasons for each candidate — this makes the selection decision auditable and reproducible months later.
